case2001.csv: In 1846 the Donner and Reed families left Springfield, Illinois, for California by covered wagon. In July, the Donner Party, as it became known, reached Fort Bridger, Wyoming. There its leaders decided to attempt a new and untested rote to the Sacramento Valley. Having reached its full size of 87 people and 20 wagons, the party was delayed by a difficult crossing of the Wasatch Range and again in the crossing of the desert west of the Great Salt Lake. The group became stranded in the eastern Sierra Nevada mountains when the region was hit by heavy snows in late October. By the time the last survivor was rescued on April 21, 1847, 40 of the 87 members had died from famine and exposure to extreme cold. (Grayson 1990)
This data frame contains the ages and sexes of the adult (over 15 years) survivors and nonsurvivors of the Donner party.

Age: Age of person
Sex: Sex of person
Status: Whether the person survived or died

case2002.csv: A 1972–1981 health survey in The Hague, Netherlands, discovered an association between keeping pet birds and increased risk of lung cancer. To investigate birdkeeping as a risk factor, researchers conducted a case–control study of patients in 1985 at four hospitals in The Hague (population 450,000). They identified 49 cases of lung cancer among the patients who were registered with a general practice, who were age 65 or younger and who had resided in the city since 1965. They also selected 98 controls from a population of residents having the same general age structure. (Holst et al. 1988)

LC: Whether subject has lung cancer
FM: Sex of subject
SS: Socioeconomic status, determined by occupation of the household’s principal wage earner
BK: Indicator for birdkeeping (caged birds in the home for more that 6 consecutive months from 5 to 14 years before diagnosis (cases) or examination (control))
AG: Age of subject (in years)
YR: Years of smoking prior to diagnosis or examination
CD: Average rate of smoking (in cigarettes per day)

ex2011.csv: This data frame contains the launch temperatures (degrees Fahrenheit) and an indicator of O-ring failures for 24 space shuttle launches prior to the space shuttle Challenger disaster of January 28, 1986.

Temperature: Launch temperature (in degrees Fahrenheit)
Failure: Indicator of O-ring failure

ex2012.csv: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD) is a genetically transmitted disease, passed from a mother to her children. Boys with the disease usually die at a young age; but affected girls usually do not suffer symptoms, may unknowingly carry the disease and may pass it to their offspring. It is believed that about 1 in 3,300 women are DMD carriers. A woman might suspect she is a carrier when a related male child develops the disease. Doctors must rely on some kind of test to detect the presence of the disease. This data frame contains data on two enzymes in the blood, creatine kinase (CK) and hemopexin (H) for 38 known DMD carriers and 82 women who are not carriers. It is desired to use these data to obtain an equation for indicating whether a women is a likely carrier. (Andrews and Herzberg 2012)

Group: Indicator whether the woman has DMD ("Case") or not ("Control")
CK: Creatine kinase reading
H: Hemopexin reading

ex2015.csv: A study examined the association between nesting locations of the Northern Spotted Owl and availability of mature forests. Wildlife biologists identified 30 nest sites. The researchers selected 30 other sites at random coordinates in the same forest. On the basis of aerial photographs, the percentage of mature forest (older than 80 years) was measured in various rings around each of the 60 sites. (Ripple et al. 1991)

Site: Site, a factor with levels "Random" and "Nest"
PctRing1: Percentage of mature forest in ring with outer radius 0.91 km
PctRing2: Percentage of mature forest in ring with outer radius 1.18 km
PctRing3: Percentage of mature forest in ring with outer radius 1.40 km
PctRing4: Percentage of mature forest in ring with outer radius 1.60 km
PctRing5: Percentage of mature forest in ring with outer radius 1.77 km
PctRing6: Percentage of mature forest in ring with outer radius 2.41 km
PctRing7: Percentage of mature forest in ring with outer radius 3.38 km

ex2016.csv: Hermon Bumpus analysed various characteristics of some house sparrows that were found on the ground after a severe winter storm in 1898. Some of the sparrows survived and some perished. This data set contains the survival status, age, the length from tip of beak to tip of tail (in mm), the alar extent (length from tip to tip of the extended wings, in mm), the weight in grams, the length of the head in mm, the length of the humerus (arm bone, in inches), the length of the femur (thigh bones, in inches), the length of the tibio–tarsus (leg bone, in inches), the breadth of the skull in inches and the length of the sternum in inches.

Status: Survival status, factor with levels "Perished" and "Survived"
AG: a numerical code corresponding to two categories of age, with 1 = “adult” and 2 = “juvenile”
TL: total length (in mm)
AE: alar extent (in mm)
WT: weight (in grams)
BH: length of beak and head (in mm)
HL: length of humerus (in inches)
FL: length of femur (in inches)
TT: length of tibio–tarsus (in inches)
SK: width of skull (in inches)
KL: length of keel of sternum (in inches)

ex2017.csv: The Catholic church has explicitly opposed authoritarian rule in some (but not all) Latin American countries. Although such action could be explained as a desire to counter repression or to increase the quality of life of its parishioners, A.J. Gill supplies evidence that the underlying reason may be competition from evangelical Protestant denominations. He compiled the data given in this data frame. (Gill 1994)

Stance: Catholic church stance, factor with levels "Pro" and "Anti"
Country: Latin American country
PQLI: Physical Quality of Life Index in the mid-1970s; Average of live expectancy at age 1, infant mortality and literacy at age 15+.
Repression: Average civil rights score for the period of authoritarian rule until 1979
Competition: Percentage increase of competitive religious groups during the period 1900–1970

ex2018.csv: The Ford Explorer is a popular sports utility vehicle made in the United States and sold throughout the world. Early in its production concern arose over a potential accident risk associated with tires of the prescribed size when the vehicle was carrying heavy loads, but the risk was thought to be acceptable if a low tire pressure was recommended. The problem was apparently exacerbated by a particular type of Firestone tire that was overly prone to separation, especially in warm temperatures. This type of tire was a common one used on Explorers in model years 1995 and later. By the end of 1999 more than 30 lawsuits had been filed over accidents that were thought to be associated with this problem. U.S. federal data on fatal car accidents were analysed at that time, showing that the odds of a fatal accident being associated with tire failure were three times as great for Explorers as for other sports utility vehicles.
Additional data from 1999 and additional variables may be used to further explore the odds ratio. It is of interest to see whether the odds that a fatal accident is tire-related depend on whether the vehicle is a Ford, after accounting for age of the car and number of passengers. Since the Ford tire problem may be due to the load carried, there is some interest in seeing whether the odds associated with a Ford depend on the number of passengers.
This data frame contains data on 1995 and later model compact sports utility vehicles involved in fatal accidents in the United States between 1995 and 1999, excluding those that were struck by another car and excluding accidents that, according to police reports, involved alcohol.

Make: Type of sports utility vehicle, factor with levels "Other" and "Ford"
VehicleAge: Vehicle age (in years); surrogate for age of tires
Passengers: Number of passengers
Cause: Cause of fatal accident, factor with levels "NotTire" and "Tire"

ex2019.csv: Following a successful test of an anti-ballistic missile (ABM) in July 1999, many prominent U.S. politicians called for the early deployment of a full ABM system. The scientific community was less enthusiastic about the efficacy of such a system. This data set contains the success or failure of ABM tests between March 1983 and May 1995. Do these data suggest any improvement in ABM test success probability over time? (Lewis et al. 1999)

Date: date of an ABM test
Months: number of months after March 1983
Result: a factor with levels "Failure" and "Success"

title: Logistic Regression
text:
library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
library(sandwich)
library(lmtest)
library(brglm2)

title: Logistic Regression
text:
Issues

Econometricians and psychometricians love the LPM because of its ease of interpretability.
Statisticians typically do not like it (though I’m agnostic).
The possible values of \(\beta_0 + \beta_1X_{i1} + \beta_2X_{i2} + \cdots + \beta_{p-1}X_{i,p-1}\) are any real numbers between \(-\infty\) and \(\infty\), but the probability of \(Y_i\) being 1 can only be between 0 and 1.
So it is possible (and typical in many datasets) to have probability estimates that are negative or greater than 1. This makes folks squeamish.
E.g., the point-wise confidence bands from the Donner party make no sense:

ggplot(donner, aes(x = Age, y = Survived)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x)








When the probability of a “1” is between about 0.2 and 0.8, then the linear and logistic (see below) models produce about the same results.

This is a conditional probability. So all values of \(E[Y|X]\) must be between 0.2 and 0.8 for all values of \(X\) for this to hold.
I.e., you cannot just look at the marginal proportions of 1’s and 0’s and use this to say if the LPM is good.

Let’s look at the differences in the Donner party example:

ggplot(donner, aes(x = Age, y = Survived)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, formula = y ~ x) +
  geom_smooth(method = "glm",
              se = FALSE,
              method.args = list(family = "binomial"),
              color = "red",
              lty = 2,
              formula = y ~ x)

title: Logistic Regression
text:
Generalized Linear Model

This is an example of a “generalized linear model”. Let \(Y_i\) follow any distribution we specify Let \(\mu_i\) be the mean of \(Y_i\). Then a generalized linear model is \[
g(\mu_i) = \beta_0 + \beta_1X_{i1} + \beta_2X_{i2} + \cdots \beta_{p-1}X_{i,p-1}
\]
Here, \(g(\cdot)\) is called the “link function”. The model is linear on the link scale (hence “generalized linear model”).
In logistic regression, \(Y_i \sim Bern(\mu_i)\) and \(g(\mu_i) = \text{logit}(\mu_i)\).
Another example is probit regression (more popular in econ) where \(g(\mu_i) = \Phi^{-1}(\mu_i)\), the standard normal quantile function (qnorm()). This gives you almost identical results (except in the tails), but statisticians don’t like the probit because it’s less interpretable than the logit.










Technical, unimportant note: The above plots the quantile function of a \(N(0, \pi^2/3)\) distribution for the probit to account for the difference in variances between the normal and logit distributions. This makes the two curves comparable.

In log-linear models (which we might discuss later), we model counts with \(Y_i \sim Poi(\mu_i)\) (Poisson Distribution) and \(g(\mu_i) = \log(\mu_i)\).

title: Logistic Regression
text:
Maximum Likelihood Estimation

We don’t use least squares for estimation. Rather, we use Maximum Likelihood.
The probability that \(Y_i = 1\) is \(p_i\) and the probability that \(Y_i = 0\) is \(1 - p_i\).
Let \(y_i \in \{0,1\}\) be the observed value of the random variable \(Y_i\). Then we can succinctly write the probabilities of \(Y_i\) as \[
Pr(Y_i = y_i) = p_i^{y_i}(1-p_i)^{1 - y_i}
\] Go ahead and plug in \(y_i = 0\) and \(y_i = 1\) to see this.
So the probability of our data given \(p_i\) is \[
Pr(Y_1 = y_1, Y_2 = y_2, \ldots, Y_n = y_n) = \prod_{i=1}^n p_i^{y_i}(1-p_i)^{1-y_i}
\]
Recall that \[
\text{logit}(p_i) = \beta_0 + \beta_1X_{i1} + \cdots \beta_{p-1}X_{i,p-1}.
\]
So the probability of the data is a function of \(\beta_0,\beta_1,\ldots,\beta_{p-1}\).
IDEA: Choose the \(\beta_k\)’s that make our observed data as probable as possible.

PHILOSOPHY: we observed our data (that’s why we have it), so it is probably likely to have been generated. Choose the parameters that would maximize the probability of seeing the data we actually saw.

The objective function is called the “likelihood”.
The resulting estimates are called the “maximum likelihood estimates” (MLE’s).
Typically, we actually maximize the log of the likelihood \[
L(\mathbf{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^n [y_i\log(p_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1-p_i)]
\]
Below, the expit curve is morphing according to different values of \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\). The text by the points is the probability of the those observed points, which in this case is just the height (when \(Y_i = 1\)) or one minus the height (when \(Y_i = 0\)) of the expit function. The likelihood is the product of those probabilities, and the log-likelihood is the sum of the log of those probabilities.

title: Logistic Regression
text:
Implementation in R

You should transform your binary variable to have 0’s and 1’s. You should call your new variable the value when \(Y_i = 1\). E.g. in the Donner party we created the “Survived” variable.

donner <- mutate(donner, Survived = if_else(Status == "Survived", 1, 0))

Let \(Y_i\) be an indicator for survival for individual \(i\), let \(X_{i1}\) be the age of individual \(i\), and let \(X_{i2}\) be an indicator for male Then we fit the model \[\begin{align}
  Y_i &\sim Bern(p_i)\\
  \text{logit}(p_i) &= \beta_0 + \beta_1X_{i1} + \beta_2X_{i2}
  \end{align}\]
We fit all generalized linear models in R with glm(). The arguments are

formula: The same idea as in lm(). Response variable on the left of the tilde, explanatory variables on the right of the tilde.
data: The data frame where the variables are.
family: The distribution of \(Y_i\).

gaussian will assume normal errors (like in lm()).
binomial in this case will fit the bernoulli model since \(Bern(p_i) = Bin(1, p_i)\).

You can specify the link function here as well. So for probit models you would do binomial(link = "probit"). The default is for logistic regression, so you just need to say “binomial” for this argument.

Let’s fit the model

glm_out <- glm(Survived ~ Age + Sex, data = donner, family = binomial)

You again use tidy() from the {broom} package to get estimates / standard errors

tidy(glm_out)

# A tibble: 3 × 5
  term        estimate std.error statistic p.value
  <chr>          <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>
1 (Intercept)   3.23      1.39        2.33  0.0198
2 Age          -0.0782    0.0373     -2.10  0.0359
3 SexMale      -1.60      0.755      -2.11  0.0345


So the estimated regression surface is \[
\text{logit}(p_i) = 3.23 -0.08X_{1} - 1.60X_{2}
\]
Exercise: What if \(X_2\) would have been an indicator for female? What would the coefficient estimate of \(\beta_2\) be?

title: Logistic Regression
text:
Bias reduction

For small sample sizes, the MLE’s of the logistic regression coefficients can be biased. A quick way to reduce this bias is through the {brglm2} package, which uses the method of Firth (1993).
Let’s try this out on the Donner party example

glm_out2 <- glm(Survived ~ Age + Sex,
                data = donner,
                family = binomial,
                method = brglm2::brglm_fit)
tidy(glm_out2, conf.int = TRUE)

# A tibble: 3 × 7
  term        estimate std.error statistic p.value conf.low conf.high
  <chr>          <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>
1 (Intercept)   2.80      1.28        2.18  0.0292    0.283  5.31    
2 Age          -0.0669    0.0346     -1.94  0.0528   -0.135  0.000821
3 SexMale      -1.43      0.725      -1.98  0.0482   -2.85  -0.0116  


This actually resulted in some moderate differences!

title: Logistic Regression
text:
Odds

The interpretation of the logistic regression model requires you to be familiar with odds.
As I write this, the Baltimore Ravens have 3 to 2 odds of defeating the Miami Dolphins tomorrow. This means that the odds of a Ravens victory are \(3/2 = 1.5\).
The odds of an event are the probability of that event divided by the probability of not that event.
Let \(p\) be the probability of an event, and let \(\omega\) be the odds of the same event. Then we have the relations: \[
\omega = \frac{p}{1-p}\\
p = \frac{\omega}{1+\omega}
\]
So odds are just another description of probabilities.
In the football example above, the Ravens’ probability of wining is 0.6, so the odds are 0.6/0.4 = 3/2 = 1.5.
Exercise: The Chiefs have 2 to 1 odds over the Chargers tomorrow. What’s the probability of a Chief’s win?
Exercise: As I write this, FiveThirtyEight has the Democrats with a 2/3 probability of keeping the Senate in the 2022 midterm election. What are the odds of a Democrat victory?

title: Logistic Regression
text:
Interpreting Logistic Regression Model

Let \(\omega_{old}\) be the odds of an individual. We can write the logistic regression model as \[
\omega_{old} = \exp\left(\beta_0 + \beta_1X_{1} + \beta_2X_{2} + \cdots \beta_{p-1}X_{p-1}\right)
\]
Suppose that a different individual has the exact same predictor values except one unit higher \(X_1\). Then \[\begin{align}
  \omega_{new} &= \exp\left(\beta_0 + \beta_1(X_{1} + 1) + \beta_2X_{2} + \cdots \beta_{p-1}X_{p-1}\right)\\
  &= \exp\left(\beta_0 + \beta_1X_{1} + \beta_1 + \beta_2X_{2} + \cdots \beta_{p-1}X_{p-1}\right)\\
  &= \exp(\beta_1)\exp\left(\beta_0 + \beta_1X_{1} + \beta_2X_{2} + \cdots \beta_{p-1}X_{p-1}\right)\\
  &= \exp(\beta_1)\omega_{old}
  \end{align}\]
Thus, the odds of the second individual are \(e^{\beta_1}\) times as large as the odds of the first individual.
Different way to say this: The odds ratio for the two individuals is \(e^{\beta_1}\).
Recall the estimated regression relationship from the Donner party example: \[
\text{logit}(p_i) = 3.23 -0.08X_{1} - 1.60X_{2}
\]
So individuals of the same sex that are 10 years younger have about twice the odds of surviving (\(e^{0.0782 \times 10} = 2.186\)). Also, a woman’s odds of survival were about 5 times that of a man of the same age (\(e^{1.6} = 4.95\)).
Exercise: The nocturia data set, described here and downloadable from here, contains patient covariates that are believed to be associated with whether the individual has nocturia (wakes up to pee). Download the data, the fit of logistic regression model of nocturia on age. Interpret the resulting coefficients.

title: Why Use Two Categorical Explanatory Variables?
text: library(tidyverse)

title: Why Use Two Categorical Explanatory Variables?
text:
Reason 1: Interest in Both Variables

You are genuinely interested in the effects of both variables.
Example: Does both pressure and temperature affect espresso foam quality?
With no interactions, every observation contributes to estimating both effects.
More efficient than running two separate one-factor experiments.

title: Why Use Two Categorical Explanatory Variables?
text:
Reason 2: Detecting and Describing Interactions

The effect of the primary variable may depend on the level of a second variable.
Example: a drug’s effectiveness might differ between males and females.
Two-way ANOVA lets you test for and characterize interaction effects.
If no interaction is found, the result generalizes across all levels of the second variable.

title: Why Use Two Categorical Explanatory Variables?
text:
Reason 3: Variance Reduction via Grouping

Including a second variable can reduce residual variance, making it easier to detect an effect of the primary variable.
Analogous to using a paired design instead of a two-sample test.


Simulation
When both variables are plotted, the groups separate clearly:









If cat2 is ignored, the within-group spread looks much larger — harder to see the cat1 effect:









Residuals are smaller when cat2 is included:


Warning: The `augment()` method for objects of class `aov` is not maintained by the broom team, and is only supported through the `lm` tidier method. Please be cautious in interpreting and reporting broom output.

This warning is displayed once per session.










Grouping variable: included in the model solely to reduce variance, not because you are directly interested in it.
Include a grouping variable regardless of whether it is statistically significant.

title: Why Use Two Categorical Explanatory Variables?
section: Reason 4: Controlling for Confounders — Simpson’s Paradox
text:
Reason 4: Controlling for Confounders — Simpson’s Paradox

In observational studies, the direction or size of an effect can reverse after adjusting for another variable.
This is Simpson’s Paradox.
Arises when one group is over-represented at a “better” (or “worse”) level of the confounder.


Simulation
Within each level of the confounder, Group High is clearly above Group Low:









But aggregated (ignoring the confounder), Group Low looks higher — the effect reverses:










Group “Low” happens to be over-represented in the “B” level of the confounder — a favorable condition — which inflates its overall mean.
Real-world example: United Airlines appeared to have worse on-time performance than competitors, but United has a hub at O’Hare, which has frequent weather delays. After controlling for airport, United was competitive.
Simpson’s paradox is less likely in randomized experiments: randomization ensures no group is systematically over- or under-represented at any level of a confounder.

title: Multi-Factor ANOVA
text:
library(tidyverse)
library(broom)

title: Multi-Factor ANOVA
text:
No Interactions
Parallel lines, same pattern in every facet — no interactions at all:

title: Multi-Factor ANOVA
text:
Two-Way Interaction Between \(x\) and \(y\) Only
When only \(x\) and \(y\) interact (but \(z\) has no interaction with either), we can check different arrangements of the three variables across the axes and facets.
Facets = \(z\), color = \(y\): Non-parallel lines within each facet, but the same non-parallel pattern in every facet. The \(x\):\(y\) interaction is the same regardless of \(z\).









Facets = \(y\), color = \(z\): Parallel lines within each facet (\(x\) and \(z\) do not interact), but the gap between lines shifts across facets of \(y\) (because \(x\):\(y\) interact, so the effect of \(x\) depends on \(y\)).









Facets = \(y\), x-axis = \(z\), color = \(x\): \(x\) and \(z\) do not interact, so lines are parallel within each facet and the pattern is the same across facets of \(y\).









Facets = \(x\), x-axis = \(z\), color = \(y\): \(y\) and \(z\) do not interact, so lines are parallel. But the gap between \(y\) lines differs across facets of \(x\) — again reflecting the \(x\):\(y\) interaction.

title: Multi-Factor ANOVA
section: Two-Way Interactions Between All Pairs (No Three-Way)
text:
Two-Way Interactions Between All Pairs (No Three-Way)
When all three pairs interact but there is no three-way interaction, non-parallel lines appear within each facet, and the non-parallel pattern changes somewhat across facets — but the change itself is consistent (not dependent on the third variable in a deeper sense).

title: Multi-Factor ANOVA
text:
Three-Way Interaction
Non-parallel lines and the interaction pattern itself changes across facets:










The relationship between \(x\) and \(y\) is different at different levels of \(z\).

title: Multi-Factor ANOVA
text:
Interaction Plots
With three factors we have \(3 \times 2 = 6\) ways to assign variables to axes and facets. Looking at all of them gives a fuller picture of which interactions may be present.

ggplot(poker, aes(x = Skill, y = Cash, color = Hand, group = Hand)) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "line") +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point") +
  facet_wrap(~Limit, labeller = label_both) +
  labs(x = "Skill", y = "Mean cash ($)", color = "Hand",
       title = "x = Skill, color = Hand, facets = Limit")









ggplot(poker, aes(x = Hand, y = Cash, color = Skill, group = Skill)) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "line") +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point") +
  facet_wrap(~Limit, labeller = label_both) +
  labs(x = "Hand", y = "Mean cash ($)", color = "Skill",
       title = "x = Hand, color = Skill, facets = Limit")









ggplot(poker, aes(x = Limit, y = Cash, color = Skill, group = Skill)) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "line") +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point") +
  facet_wrap(~Hand, labeller = label_both) +
  labs(x = "Limit", y = "Mean cash ($)", color = "Skill",
       title = "x = Limit, color = Skill, facets = Hand")









ggplot(poker, aes(x = Skill, y = Cash, color = Limit, group = Limit)) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "line") +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point") +
  facet_wrap(~Hand, labeller = label_both) +
  labs(x = "Skill", y = "Mean cash ($)", color = "Limit",
       title = "x = Skill, color = Limit, facets = Hand")









ggplot(poker, aes(x = Hand, y = Cash, color = Limit, group = Limit)) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "line") +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point") +
  facet_wrap(~Skill, labeller = label_both) +
  labs(x = "Hand", y = "Mean cash ($)", color = "Limit",
       title = "x = Hand, color = Limit, facets = Skill")









ggplot(poker, aes(x = Limit, y = Cash, color = Hand, group = Hand)) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "line") +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point") +
  facet_wrap(~Skill, labeller = label_both) +
  labs(x = "Limit", y = "Mean cash ($)", color = "Hand",
       title = "x = Limit, color = Hand, facets = Skill")








Observations from the plots:

The effect of Hand on Cash appears to differ between experts and average players (non-parallel lines when Skill is on the facet or color), suggesting a Skill × Hand interaction.
The effect of Limit also appears to differ between skill levels, suggesting a Skill × Limit interaction.
The Hand × Limit interaction looks less clear.

title: Multi-Factor ANOVA
text:
Top-Down Testing Strategy
Start with the full interaction model (all main effects + all interactions). Test the three-way interaction first; if not significant, drop it and test the two-way interactions.

aout_full <- aov(Cash ~ Skill * Hand * Limit, data = poker)
tidy(aout_full)

# A tibble: 8 × 6
  term                df  sumsq meansq statistic   p.value
  <chr>            <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
1 Skill                1   49.2   49.2      2.84  9.31e- 2
2 Hand                 2 2647.  1323.      76.4   2.39e-27
3 Limit                1   31.7   31.7      1.83  1.77e- 1
4 Skill:Hand           2  219.   110.       6.32  2.05e- 3
5 Skill:Limit          1  119.   119.       6.88  9.19e- 3
6 Hand:Limit           2   97.3   48.6      2.81  6.19e- 2
7 Skill:Hand:Limit     2   42.4   21.2      1.22  2.96e- 1
8 Residuals          288 4987.    17.3     NA    NA       



The three-way interaction Skill:Hand:Limit is not significant — drop it.


aout_2way <- aov(Cash ~ Skill + Hand + Limit +
                   Skill:Hand + Skill:Limit + Hand:Limit,
                 data = poker)
tidy(aout_2way)

# A tibble: 7 × 6
  term           df  sumsq meansq statistic   p.value
  <chr>       <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
1 Skill           1   49.2   49.2      2.83  9.33e- 2
2 Hand            2 2647.  1323.      76.3   2.39e-27
3 Limit           1   31.7   31.7      1.83  1.78e- 1
4 Skill:Hand      2  219.   110.       6.31  2.07e- 3
5 Skill:Limit     1  119.   119.       6.87  9.24e- 3
6 Hand:Limit      2   97.3   48.6      2.80  6.22e- 2
7 Residuals     290 5030.    17.3     NA    NA       



Skill:Hand and Skill:Limit are significant; Hand:Limit is not.
Drop Hand:Limit and adopt the model with only those two interactions.


aout_sub <- aov(Cash ~ Skill + Hand + Limit + Skill:Hand + Skill:Limit,
                data = poker)
tidy(aout_sub)

# A tibble: 6 × 6
  term           df  sumsq meansq statistic   p.value
  <chr>       <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
1 Skill           1   49.2   49.2      2.80  9.53e- 2
2 Hand            2 2647.  1323.      75.4   4.07e-27
3 Limit           1   31.7   31.7      1.80  1.80e- 1
4 Skill:Hand      2  219.   110.       6.24  2.23e- 3
5 Skill:Limit     1  119.   119.       6.78  9.67e- 3
6 Residuals     292 5127.    17.6     NA    NA

title: Multi-Factor ANOVA
text:
Residual Plot

augment(aout_sub) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = .fitted, y = .resid)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, lty = 2, color = "red") +
  labs(x = "Fitted values", y = "Residuals")








No obvious patterns — the equal-variance assumption looks reasonable.

title: Multi-Factor ANOVA
text:
Coefficient Estimates

lm_sub <- lm(Cash ~ Skill + Hand + Limit + Skill:Hand + Skill:Limit,
             data = poker)
tidy(lm_sub)

# A tibble: 8 × 5
  term                    estimate std.error statistic  p.value
  <chr>                      <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>
1 (Intercept)                 5.40     0.684      7.89 6.04e-14
2 SkillExpert                 1.40     0.968      1.45 1.48e- 1
3 HandNeutral                 5.18     0.838      6.18 2.14e- 9
4 HandGood                    9.27     0.838     11.1  5.51e-24
5 LimitNone                  -1.91     0.684     -2.79 5.59e- 3
6 SkillExpert:HandNeutral    -1.44     1.19      -1.21 2.25e- 1
7 SkillExpert:HandGood       -4.12     1.19      -3.48 5.79e- 4
8 SkillExpert:LimitNone       2.52     0.968      2.60 9.67e- 3


Using the notation \(\alpha\) for Skill, \(\beta\) for Hand, \(\gamma\) for Limit (reference levels: Average, Bad, Fixed):



Parameter
Level

title: Multi-Factor ANOVA
text:
Interpretations
Skill × Hand interaction: How much does hand quality matter?
The mean difference between players with good hands and players with bad hands depends on skill level. For expert players:
\[\mu_{\text{Expert, Good}} - \mu_{\text{Expert, Bad}} = \beta_3 + (\alpha\beta)_{23} = 9.270 - 4.124 = 5.146\]
For average players (where \((\alpha\beta)_{13} = 0\) by definition):
\[\mu_{\text{Average, Good}} - \mu_{\text{Average, Bad}} = \beta_3 + (\alpha\beta)_{13} = 9.270 + 0 = 9.270\]
Average players gain more from receiving a good hand than experts do. Experts can partially compensate for a bad hand through skill, so hand quality matters less to them.
Skill × Limit interaction: Who benefits from a no-limit game?
The mean difference between no-limit and fixed-limit games by skill level:
\[\mu_{\text{Average, None}} - \mu_{\text{Average, Fixed}} = \gamma_2 = -1.910\]
\[\mu_{\text{Expert, None}} - \mu_{\text{Expert, Fixed}} = \gamma_2 + (\alpha\gamma)_{22} = -1.910 + 2.520 = 0.610\]
On average, removing the betting limit hurts average players (−$1.91) but helps experts (+$0.61). The no-limit format rewards skill: experts can leverage their advantage with larger bets.

title: Outliers and Influential Observations
text:
library(tidyverse)
library(broom)

title: Outliers and Influential Observations
text:
Three Concepts








Type
What makes it unusual
Effect on the fit




Outlier
Large residual — unusual \(Y\) for its \(X\) values
May or may not influence the line


High-leverage point
Unusual \(X\) values — far from the center of the predictor space
May or may not have a large residual


Influential observation
Removing it noticeably changes the fitted line
High leverage and an outlier




High leverage alone is not harmful if the point happens to fall on the regression line.
An outlier at the center of the \(X\) range tugs the intercept but not the slope.
A point with both high leverage and a large residual is the dangerous combination.

title: Outliers and Influential Observations
section: Why High Leverage Is Hard to Spot Marginally
text:
Why High Leverage Is Hard to Spot Marginally

You cannot find a multivariate outlier just by looking at histograms of each variable separately.
Example: a point at \((X_1, X_2) = (0.75, -0.17)\) below appears extreme in the 2-D cloud but looks perfectly ordinary in either histogram alone.

title: Outliers and Influential Observations
text:
Background

Researchers developed a treatment to disrupt the blood-brain barrier (BBB) so that anti-cancer drugs can reach brain tumors more effectively.
Rats with brain tumors were randomized to receive the BBB-disrupting treatment or a saline control.
Response: Ratio = drug concentration in brain / drug concentration in liver.

Higher ratio means more drug reached the brain.

Other measured variables: sacrifice time, sex, initial weight, weight loss, tumor weight, days post-inoculation.


case1102 <- read_csv("https://dcgerard.github.io/stat_302/data/case1102.csv")
case1102 <- case1102 |>
  mutate(Ratio    = Brain / Liver,
         logRatio = log(Ratio),
         logTime  = log(Time))
glimpse(case1102)

Rows: 34
Columns: 12
$ Brain     <dbl> 41081, 44286, 102926, 25927, 42643, 31342, 22815, 16629, 223…
$ Liver     <dbl> 1456164, 1602171, 1601936, 1776411, 1351184, 1790863, 163338…
$ Time      <dbl> 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 3.0, 3.0, 3.0, …
$ Treatment <chr> "BD", "BD", "BD", "BD", "BD", "NS", "NS", "NS", "NS", "BD", …
$ Days      <dbl> 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 9, 9, 10, 10…
$ Sex       <chr> "Female", "Female", "Female", "Female", "Female", "Female", …
$ Weight    <dbl> 239, 225, 224, 184, 250, 196, 200, 273, 216, 267, 263, 228, …
$ Loss      <dbl> 5.9, 4.0, -4.9, 9.8, 6.0, 7.7, 0.5, 4.0, 2.8, 2.6, 1.1, 0.0,…
$ Tumor     <dbl> 221, 246, 61, 168, 164, 260, 27, 308, 93, 73, 25, 133, 203, …
$ Ratio     <dbl> 0.02821179, 0.02764124, 0.06425101, 0.01459516, 0.03155973, …
$ logRatio  <dbl> -3.56801513, -3.58844626, -2.74495789, -4.22706542, -3.45587…
$ logTime   <dbl> -0.6931472, -0.6931472, -0.6931472, -0.6931472, -0.6931472, …

title: Outliers and Influential Observations
text:
EDA

ggplot(case1102, aes(x = logTime, y = logRatio, color = Treatment)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(x = "log(Sacrifice Time)", y = "log(Brain/Liver Ratio)")









The treatment appears to increase the brain/liver ratio — the BD-treated group sits higher.
Log transformations of both Ratio and Time make the relationship more linear and stabilize variance.

title: Outliers and Influential Observations
text:
Fitting the Rich Model

Use as.factor(logTime) to treat sacrifice time as categorical (three distinct time points were used).
Include all available variables and an interaction between treatment and time.


lm_rich <- lm(logRatio ~ as.factor(logTime) * Treatment +
                Sex + Weight + Loss + Tumor,
              data = case1102)


aug_rich <- augment(lm_rich)

ggplot(aug_rich, aes(x = .fitted, y = .resid)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(x = "Fitted values", y = "Residuals")









Two points stand out with large residuals — worth investigating further.

title: Outliers and Influential Observations
text:
Definition

Leverage measures how far an observation’s \(X\) values are from the center of the predictor space.
A high-leverage point has the potential to pull the regression line.
Leverage values (often called “hat values”) are always between 0 and 1.
A typical leverage value is \(p / n\), where \(p\) = number of parameters.
Rule of thumb: leverage \(> 2p/n\) is “high.”

title: Outliers and Influential Observations
text:
Computing Leverage in R

n <- nrow(aug_rich)
p <- length(coef(lm_rich))

aug_rich |>
  mutate(obs = row_number()) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = obs, y = .hat)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 2 * p / n, linetype = "dashed", color = "tomato") +
  labs(x = "Observation number", y = "Leverage",
       title = "Dashed line = 2p/n threshold")









Several observations exceed the \(2p/n\) threshold — worth noting, but not automatically alarming.

title: Outliers and Influential Observations
text:
Definition

Ordinary residuals have different variances depending on the observation’s leverage.
Studentized residuals correct for this: they measure how many standard deviations an observation’s residual is from zero.
Under the model assumptions, studentized residuals follow a \(t\)-distribution.
Rule of thumb: expect about 5% of studentized residuals to fall outside \(\pm 2\) by chance. Many more than 5% suggests model problems.

title: Outliers and Influential Observations
text:
Computing Studentized Residuals in R

aug_rich |>
  mutate(stud_res = rstudent(lm_rich)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = .fitted, y = stud_res)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-2, 2), linetype = "dashed", color = "tomato") +
  labs(x = "Fitted values", y = "Studentized residuals")









Two observations have studentized residuals exceeding \(\pm 2\) — consistent with what we saw in the raw residual plot.

title: Outliers and Influential Observations
text:
Definition

Cook’s distance measures the overall influence of an observation: how much do all the fitted values change when this observation is removed?
It combines both leverage and residual size into a single number.
Cook’s distance is always positive.
Rule of thumb: Cook’s distance \(> 1\) indicates a strongly influential observation.

title: Outliers and Influential Observations
text:
Computing Cook’s Distance in R

aug_rich |>
  mutate(obs = row_number()) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = obs, y = .cooksd)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "tomato") +
  labs(x = "Observation number", y = "Cook's distance",
       title = "Dashed line = rule-of-thumb threshold of 1")









No observation exceeds Cook’s distance of 1 in this dataset.
The two observations with large studentized residuals are noticeable but not enormously influential overall.

title: Outliers and Influential Observations
text:
Using augment() for Diagnostics
The augment() function from broom adds model diagnostics directly to the data frame:



Column
Description




.fitted
Fitted values \(\hat{Y}_i\)


.resid
Residuals \(Y_i - \hat{Y}_i\)


.hat
Leverage values


.std.resid
Standardized residuals (similar to studentized)


.cooksd
Cook’s distance




augment(lm_rich) |>
  select(.fitted, .resid, .hat, .std.resid, .cooksd) |>
  arrange(desc(.cooksd)) |>
  glimpse()

Rows: 34
Columns: 5
$ .fitted    <dbl> 0.7525731, 0.5031866, -4.1148668, 1.2177072, 0.7879681, 1.9…
$ .resid     <dbl> 1.15473188, -1.39119508, -0.46339887, -0.53456014, 0.771023…
$ .hat       <dbl> 0.4978708, 0.3749781, 0.5863339, 0.4619573, 0.3251558, 0.43…
$ .std.resid <dbl> 2.9551482, -3.1911428, -1.3065796, -1.3215817, 1.7020472, -…
$ .cooksd    <dbl> 0.721569998, 0.509121954, 0.201644148, 0.124965985, 0.11631…

title: Outliers and Influential Observations
text:
Step 1: Investigate

Look at the actual data values for high-influence points.
Is there a data entry error? A species misclassified? An impossible value?
If so, correct or remove and document why.

title: Outliers and Influential Observations
text:
Step 2: Fit With and Without

If no data error is found, do not automatically discard the point.
Fit your final model both including and excluding the influential observations.

If conclusions do not change: keep them, report the full analysis.
If conclusions change substantially: report both results and note the sensitivity.

State the scope of inference — e.g., “Results apply to rats weighing between 200 and 400 g.”

title: Outliers and Influential Observations
text:
Step 3: Never Cherry-Pick

Removing an observation purely because it disagrees with your hypothesis is scientific misconduct.
Document every exclusion decision and your reason for it.

title: F-tests for Nested Models
text: library(tidyverse)

title: F-tests for Nested Models
text:
The Problem with Individual \(t\)-tests

In multiple regression we can test each coefficient with a \(t\)-test.
But some variables enter the model through multiple terms (e.g., \(X\) and \(X^2\)).
Testing \(\beta_1 = 0\) and \(\beta_2 = 0\) separately does not answer “does \(X\) matter at all?”
We need to test \(H_0: \beta_1 = 0\) and \(\beta_2 = 0\) simultaneously — a joint test.

title: F-tests for Nested Models
text:
Case Study: Kentucky Derby

The Kentucky Derby is a famous horse race held annually since 1875.
We have the winning speed (mph) and the year of each race.
Does year explain speed? (Speed has increased over time as breeding and track conditions improved.)


ex0920 <- read_csv("https://dcgerard.github.io/stat_302/data/ex0920.csv")
glimpse(ex0920)

Rows: 116
Columns: 8
$ Year        <dbl> 1896, 1897, 1898, 1899, 1900, 1901, 1902, 1903, 1904, 1905…
$ Winner      <chr> "Ben Brush", "Typhoon II", "Plaudit", "Manuel", "Lieut. Gi…
$ Starters    <dbl> 8, 6, 4, 5, 7, 5, 4, 6, 5, 3, 6, 6, 8, 10, 7, 7, 7, 8, 7, …
$ NetToWinner <dbl> 4850, 4850, 4850, 4850, 4850, 4850, 4850, 4850, 4850, 4850…
$ Time        <dbl> 127.75, 132.50, 129.00, 132.00, 126.25, 127.75, 128.75, 12…
$ Speed       <dbl> 35.23, 33.96, 34.88, 34.09, 35.64, 35.23, 34.95, 34.88, 35…
$ Track       <chr> "Dusty", "Heavy", "Good", "Fast", "Fast", "Fast", "Fast", …
$ Conditions  <chr> "Fast", "Slow", "Fast", "Fast", "Fast", "Fast", "Fast", "F…



ggplot(ex0920, aes(x = Year, y = Speed)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE) +
  labs(y = "Winning Speed (mph)")









The trend is clearly nonlinear: speed rose sharply in the early 20th century then leveled off.
A quadratic model is appropriate: \[\mu(\text{Speed} \mid \text{Year}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,\text{Year} + \beta_2\,\text{Year}^2\]
Question: Is year at all associated with winning speed?
\(H_0: \beta_1 = 0\) and \(\beta_2 = 0\)
\(H_A: \beta_1 \neq 0\) or \(\beta_2 \neq 0\)

title: F-tests for Nested Models
text:
Definition

A model is nested inside another if it can be obtained by setting some coefficients in the larger model to zero.
The larger model is the full model.
The smaller model is the reduced model.

title: F-tests for Nested Models
text:
Kentucky Derby Example







Model
Equation




Full
\(\mu(\text{Speed} \mid \text{Year}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,\text{Year} + \beta_2\,\text{Year}^2\)


Reduced (under \(H_0\))
\(\mu(\text{Speed}) = \beta_0\)




The reduced model is the full model with \(\beta_1 = \beta_2 = 0\).

title: F-tests for Nested Models
text:
Non-Nested Models (Counter-example)

Model A: \(\mu(\text{Speed} \mid \text{Year}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,\text{Year} + \beta_2\,\text{Year}^2\)
Model B: \(\mu(\text{Speed} \mid \text{Starters}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,\text{Starters} + \beta_2\,\text{Starters}^2\)
Neither is a special case of the other — cannot use an \(F\)-test.
For non-nested models, use AIC or BIC (see the model selection lecture).

title: F-tests for Nested Models
text:
Intuition: Residuals Tell the Story

If the full model fits much better (smaller residuals), we have evidence against \(H_0\).
We compare the residual sum of squares (RSS) under each model.


ex0920 <- ex0920 |> mutate(Year2 = Year^2)
lm_full    <- lm(Speed ~ Year + Year2, data = ex0920)
lm_reduced <- lm(Speed ~ 1,            data = ex0920)

ex0920 <- ex0920 |>
  mutate(fit_full = fitted(lm_full),
         fit_red  = fitted(lm_reduced))




















The full model fits much better — the residuals are far smaller.
This large difference in fit is strong evidence against \(H_0\).

title: F-tests for Nested Models
text:
Key Quantities
Let \(p_F\) = number of parameters in the full model, \(p_R\) = number in the reduced, \(n\) = sample size.







Symbol
Meaning




\(RSS_F\)
Sum of squared residuals under the full model


\(RSS_R\)
Sum of squared residuals under the reduced model


\(ESS\)
Extra sum of squares \(= RSS_R - RSS_F\) (how much better the full fits)


\(df_{\text{extra}}\)
\(p_F - p_R\) (extra parameters in the full model)


\(df_F\)
\(n - p_F\) (residual degrees of freedom for the full model)

title: F-tests for Nested Models
text:
Formula and Distribution
\[F = \frac{ESS / \, df_{\text{extra}}}{RSS_F / \, df_F}\]

Numerator: average extra variability explained per additional parameter.
Denominator: average residual variability per degree of freedom (an estimate of \(\sigma^2\)).
Under \(H_0\): \(F\) follows an \(F\)-distribution with \((df_{\text{extra}},\, df_F)\) degrees of freedom.
A large \(F\) means the full model explains substantially more — evidence against \(H_0\).

title: F-tests for Nested Models
text:
Reading the Output



Column
Meaning




Res.Df
Residual degrees of freedom for each model


RSS
Residual sum of squares for each model


Df
\(df_{\text{extra}} = p_F - p_R\)


Sum of Sq
\(ESS = RSS_R - RSS_F\)


F
\(F\)-statistic


Pr(>F)
\(p\)-value




Conclusion: We have overwhelming evidence that year is associated with winning speed (\(F = 104.6\) on 2 and 119 df, \(p < 0.001\)).

title: F-tests for Nested Models
text:
Setup

Starters: number of horses that started the race.
After accounting for a quadratic trend in year, does the number of starters add anything?

\[H_0: \beta_3 = \beta_4 = 0 \text{ in:}\] \[\mu(\text{Speed} \mid \text{Year}, \text{Starters}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,\text{Year} + \beta_2\,\text{Year}^2
  + \beta_3\,\text{Starters} + \beta_4\,\text{Starters}^2\]

Full: Year + Year² + Starters + Starters²
Reduced: Year + Year² (same as the full model above)


ex0920 <- ex0920 |> mutate(Starters2 = Starters^2)
lm_full2    <- lm(Speed ~ Year + Year2 + Starters + Starters2, data = ex0920)
lm_reduced2 <- lm(Speed ~ Year + Year2,                        data = ex0920)
anova(lm_reduced2, lm_full2)

Analysis of Variance Table

Model 1: Speed ~ Year + Year2
Model 2: Speed ~ Year + Year2 + Starters + Starters2
  Res.Df    RSS Df Sum of Sq      F  Pr(>F)  
1    113 33.083                              
2    111 30.903  2    2.1803 3.9156 0.02274 *
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1



Conclusion: We do have some evidence that the number of starters is associated with speed after adjusting for year (\(F = 3.92\) on 2 and 111 df, \(p = 0.023\)).

title: Multiple Regression
text:
library(tidyverse)
library(broom)

title: Multiple Regression
text:
A Useful Notation

\(\mu(\text{brain} \mid \text{gestation}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,\text{gestation}\)

The mean of brain weight equals \(\beta_0\) plus \(\beta_1\) times the gestation time.

\(\mu(\text{brain} \mid \text{gestation},\,\text{body}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,\text{gestation} + \beta_2\,\text{body}\)

The mean of brain weight equals \(\beta_0\) plus \(\beta_1\) times the gestation time plus \(\beta_2\) times the body weight.

title: Multiple Regression
text:
Interpretation Changes with the Model
The interpretation of \(\beta_1\) changes when the model changes, because the comparison group changes:

\(\mu(\text{brain} \mid \text{gestation}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,\text{gestation}\)

\(\beta_1\) is the mean difference in brain weight comparing gestation periods 1 day apart in the population of all mammal species.

\(\mu(\text{brain} \mid \text{gestation},\,\text{body}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,\text{gestation} + \beta_2\,\text{body}\)

\(\beta_1\) is the mean difference comparing gestation periods 1 day apart in subpopulations with the same body weight.

title: Multiple Regression
text:
Visualizing the Coefficient Interpretation
Consider artificial data with \(\beta_0 = 2\), \(\beta_1 = 1\) (positive \(X_1\) effect), \(\beta_2 = -3\) (large negative \(X_2\) effect), where \(X_1\) and \(X_2\) are positively correlated.

beta0 <- 2; beta1 <- 1; beta2 <- -3
x2 <- rep(1:10, 5)
x1 <- rep(0:9, 5) + rep(1:5, each = 10)
y  <- beta0 + beta1 * x1 + beta2 * x2
df_fake <- data.frame(x1 = x1, y = y, x2 = as.factor(x2))

Without \(X_2\) in the model — the marginal slope looks negative even though \(\beta_1 = 1\):

ggplot(df_fake, aes(x = x1, y = y)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  theme(axis.text = element_blank(), axis.ticks = element_blank())








With \(X_2\) color-coded — the slope within each level of \(X_2\) is positive (\(= \beta_1 = 1\)), and the parallel lines sit at different heights:

ggplot(df_fake, aes(x = x1, y = y, color = x2)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  theme(axis.text = element_blank(), axis.ticks = element_blank()) +
  guides(color = guide_legend(keywidth = 0.1, keyheight = 0.1,
                               default.unit = "inch"))








The figure below shows the same idea using the real brain-size data (case0902):

Left: a single marginal fit line for all 96 species (\(\hat\beta_1 \approx 2.23\)).
Middle: points colored by log(Body) tertile — large-bodied species (green) drive much of the steep marginal slope because they have both long gestations and large brains.
Right: parallel fit lines from the additive model — every body-weight group has the same gestation slope (\(\hat\beta_1 \approx 0.67\)) but sits at a different height, reflecting the body-size effect \(\beta_2\).

title: Multiple Regression
text:
Interpreting \(p\)-values
The interpretation of significance depends on what other variables are in the model.
\(\mu(\text{brain} \mid \text{gestation}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,\text{gestation}\)

\(H_0: \beta_1 = 0\) vs \(H_A: \beta_1 \neq 0\)
Model under \(H_0\): \(\mu(\text{brain}) = \beta_0\)
Model under \(H_A\): \(\mu(\text{brain} \mid \text{gestation}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,\text{gestation}\)
If we reject \(H_0\): “We have strong evidence that gestation is related to brain weight.”
If we fail to reject \(H_0\): “We do not have strong evidence that gestation is related to brain weight.”

\(\mu(\text{brain} \mid \text{gestation},\,\text{body}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,\text{gestation} + \beta_2\,\text{body}\)

\(H_0: \beta_1 = 0\) vs \(H_A: \beta_1 \neq 0\)
Model under \(H_0\): \(\mu(\text{brain} \mid \text{body}) = \beta_0 + \beta_2\,\text{body}\)
Model under \(H_A\): \(\mu(\text{brain} \mid \text{gestation},\,\text{body}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,\text{gestation} + \beta_2\,\text{body}\)
If we reject \(H_0\): “We have strong evidence that gestation is related to brain weight after adjusting for body size.”
If we fail to reject \(H_0\): “We do not have strong evidence that gestation is related to brain weight after adjusting for body size.”

title: Linear Model Assumptions Worksheet
text:
Wine Consumption and Heart Disease
Data frame ex0823 from the Sleuth3 package contains the average wine consumption rates (in liters per person) and number of ischemic heart disease deaths (per 1,000 men aged 55 to 64 years old) for 18 industrial countries. Do these data suggest that the heart disease death rate is associated with average wine consumption? If so, how can that relationship be described? Do any countries have substantially higher or lower death rates than others with similar wine consumption rates? We will answer these questions.
Load the data with:

library(tidyverse)
ex0823 <- read_csv("https://dcgerard.github.io/stat_302/data/ex0823.csv")
glimpse(ex0823)

Rows: 18
Columns: 3
$ Country   <chr> "Norway", "Scotland", "England", "Ireland", "Finland", "Cana…
$ Wine      <dbl> 2.8, 3.2, 3.2, 3.4, 4.3, 4.9, 5.1, 5.2, 5.9, 5.9, 6.6, 8.3, …
$ Mortality <dbl> 6.2, 9.0, 7.1, 6.8, 10.2, 7.8, 9.3, 5.9, 8.9, 5.5, 7.1, 9.1,…


1. What is the explanatory variable? What is the response variable? What are the observational units? Is this an observational study or a randomized experiment?
2. Make a scatterplot of Wine (x-axis) vs Mortality (y-axis). Describe the relationship — does it look linear? Comment on the direction and strength of any trend you see.
3. Based on the scatterplot, which of the four linear model assumptions (linearity, independence, equal variance, normality) appear potentially violated? Explain briefly.
4. Fit an initial simple linear regression of Mortality on Wine using lm(). Then create a residual plot (fitted values on the \(x\)-axis, residuals on the \(y\)-axis) using augment() from the broom package.
5. Examine the residual plot. What patterns, if any, do you see? Which assumption(s) does this suggest are violated? What transformation(s) might address these issues?
6. Try the following transformations one at a time and evaluate each with a scatterplot and residual plot:

Log-transform Mortality only: fit log(Mortality) ~ Wine.
Log-transform Wine only: fit Mortality ~ log(Wine).
Log-transform both variables: fit log(Mortality) ~ log(Wine).

For each, note whether the residual plot looks better or worse than the original. Which combination, if any, satisfies the linearity and equal-variance assumptions?
7. Using your final model from Question 6, report the estimated slope coefficient. Then compute the appropriate back-transformed estimate and 95% confidence interval. Write a one-sentence plain-language interpretation of the slope on the original scale, in the context of this study.
8. Is it appropriate to use causal language such as “drinking more wine causes lower heart disease mortality”? Explain your reasoning based on the study design.

title: Simple Linear Regression Worksheet
text:
LSD
In Wagner et al. (1968), the authors intravenously injected d-lysergic acid diethylamide (better known as LSD) into seven volunteer male subjects. They then instructed the subjects to solve a set of simple arithmetic problems as quickly as possible during a three-minute period. Load the data with:

lsd <- read_csv("https://dcgerard.github.io/stat_302/data/lsd.csv")
lsd

# A tibble: 7 × 2
   conc score
  <dbl> <dbl>
1  1.17  78.9
2  2.97  58.2
3  3.26  67.5
4  4.69  37.5
5  5.83  45.6
6  6     32.9
7  6.41  30.0


The variables are: conc, the tissue concentration of LSD (nanograms per mL); and score, their performance score on the arithmetic questions as a percent of a control score.
1. What is the explanatory variable? What is the response variable? What are the observational units?
2. Make a scatterplot of score vs. conc. Does a linear model appear appropriate?
3. Write out the simple linear regression model for these data. Define each term in the context of the study. What causal language (if any) is appropriate for the slope interpretation, and why?
4. What assumptions does simple linear regression make about the distribution of the explanatory variable?
5. Fit a linear model of score on conc using lm().
6. Make a residual plot (fitted values on the \(x\)-axis, residuals on the \(y\)-axis). Does the constant-variance assumption look reasonable?
7. State the null and alternative hypotheses to test whether concentration is associated with score.
8. Use tidy() from the broom package to test the hypotheses from Question 7. Report the test statistic, \(p\)-value, and a 95% confidence interval for the slope. State a conclusion in plain language, including an interpretation of the slope estimate.
9. Suppose a new individual has a tissue concentration of 4 ng/mL. Compute their predicted test score.
10. Compute a 95% prediction interval for that individual’s score. What range of scores is plausible for a single person at that concentration?
11. As the sample size approaches infinity, what does the standard error of a prediction approach? Explain why.
12. A fellow researcher asks you to predict the score for an individual with a concentration of 9 ng/mL. What concern would you raise?








References

Wagner, J. G., G. K. Aghajanian, and O. H. L. Bing. 1968. “Correlation of Performance Test Scores with "Tissue Concentration" of Lysergic Acid Diethylamide in Human Subjects.” Clinical Pharmacology & Therapeutics 9 (5): 635–38. https://doi.org/10.1002/cpt196895635.

title: Chapter 6 Worksheet
text:
case0502 <- read_csv("https://dcgerard.github.io/stat_302/data/case0502.csv") |>
  mutate(Judge = factor(Judge))
sardine <- read_csv("https://dcgerard.github.io/stat_302/data/sardine.csv") |>
  mutate(location = factor(location,
                           labels = c("Alaska", "Brit. Columbia",
                                      "San Francisco", "Monterey",
                                      "San Pedro", "San Diego")))


Linear Combinations (Spock Data)
1. In the Spock data, suppose researchers wanted — before looking at the data — to test whether the three judges with the highest average percent-women in the dataset (A, B, E) have a different mean from the three with the lowest (C, D, F), ignoring Spock’s judge entirely.

Write out the coefficient vector for this contrast (using the factor level order A, B, C, D, E, F, Spock’s). Does it qualify as a contrast?
Use linearHypothesis() to test this hypothesis. Report the \(F\)-statistic, \(p\)-value, and your conclusion.

2. The hypothesis \(H_0: \mu_A = \mu_B\) is a pairwise comparison — a special case of a linear combination.

What is the coefficient vector (in the order A, B, C, D, E, F, Spock’s)?
Verify using linearHypothesis() that the \(p\)-value matches what pairwise.t.test() gives (with p.adjust.method = "none").

3. What is the estimated value of \(\gamma\) for the contrast that compares Spock’s mean to the average of the other six judges? Interpret this number in the context of the study.
4. Compute a 95% confidence interval for \(\gamma\) (Spock’s mean minus the average of the other six means) using the formula \(\hat{\gamma} \pm t_{n-I}(0.975) \cdot SE(\hat\gamma)\). What does this interval tell you about how different Spock’s judge is?



Multiple Comparisons (Sardine Data)
These exercises use vertebrae counts from Pacific sardines sampled at six locations along the Pacific coast. (Clarke 1936)
5. Make side-by-side boxplots of number (vertebrae count) by location. Do you think the equal-variance and normality assumptions are satisfied well enough to proceed with ANOVA? Explain.
6. Run the omnibus \(F\)-test with aov(). How many pairwise comparisons would be needed to test all pairs of locations? How many spurious rejections would you expect at \(\alpha = 0.05\) if all location means were truly equal?
7. Run all pairwise \(t\)-tests with no \(p\)-value adjustment. Which pairs have a raw \(p\)-value below 0.05?
8. Apply the Tukey-Kramer adjustment with TukeyHSD(). Which pairs now show evidence of a difference? Produce the confidence-interval plot. How do the results compare to the unadjusted tests?
9. Apply the Holm adjustment using pairwise.t.test(). Compare these adjusted \(p\)-values to the Tukey-Kramer values — are the conclusions the same?
10. Suppose researchers had suspected before collecting data that sardines from colder waters (Alaska and British Columbia) have more vertebrae than those from warmer waters (San Francisco, Monterey, San Pedro, San Diego). This is a single pre-planned contrast.

Write the null hypothesis in terms of the six location means \(\mu_1, \ldots, \mu_6\).
Check the factor level order of location, then set up the coefficient vector.

11. Test the planned contrast from Exercise 10 using linearHypothesis() (refit with ~ location - 1 first). Report the \(F\)-statistic, \(p\)-value, and a plain-language conclusion. Because this is a single pre-planned comparison, no multiplicity adjustment is needed.
12. Explain in your own words why the Tukey-Kramer procedure gives smaller adjusted \(p\)-values than Bonferroni for all-pairwise comparisons, while Bonferroni is more general. In which situation would you prefer Bonferroni over Tukey-Kramer?





References

Clarke, F. N. 1936. “Variations in the Number of Vertebrae in Sardine, Sardinops Caerulea (Girard).” Copeia 1936: 147–50. https://doi.org/10.2307/1435822.

title: Linear Combinations of Group Means
text:
library(tidyverse)
library(car)

title: Linear Combinations of Group Means
text:
Motivation
We want to compare Spock’s judge (\(\mu_1\)) to the average of the other six judges (\(\mu_2, \ldots, \mu_7\)), without having to assume those six means are equal. That is:
\[H_0: \mu_1 = \tfrac{1}{6}(\mu_2 + \mu_3 + \cdots + \mu_7),
\qquad
H_A: \mu_1 \neq \tfrac{1}{6}(\mu_2 + \mu_3 + \cdots + \mu_7)\]
Rewriting the null:
\[\mu_1 - \tfrac{1}{6}\mu_2 - \tfrac{1}{6}\mu_3 - \cdots - \tfrac{1}{6}\mu_7 = 0\]
The left-hand side is a linear combination of the group means.

title: Linear Combinations of Group Means
text:
Definition
A linear combination of \(\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_I\) with coefficients \(c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_I\) is:
\[\gamma = c_1\mu_1 + c_2\mu_2 + \cdots + c_I\mu_I\]
If the coefficients sum to zero (\(c_1 + c_2 + \cdots + c_I = 0\)), the linear combination is called a contrast.
For the Spock comparison: \(c_1 = 1\) and \(c_2 = c_3 = \cdots = c_7 = -\tfrac{1}{6}\). Check: \(1 + 6 \times (-\tfrac{1}{6}) = 0\). So this is a contrast.

title: Linear Combinations of Group Means
text:
Hypotheses
For any linear combination \(\gamma\), the hypotheses are:
\[H_0: \gamma = 0, \qquad H_A: \gamma \neq 0\]

title: Linear Combinations of Group Means
text:
Estimation
Estimate each \(\mu_i\) with the group sample mean \(\bar{Y}_{i\bullet}\):
\[\hat{\gamma} = c_1 \bar{Y}_{1\bullet} + c_2 \bar{Y}_{2\bullet} + \cdots + c_I \bar{Y}_{I\bullet}\]

title: Linear Combinations of Group Means
text:
Standard Error
Because the group means are independent and \(\mathrm{Var}(\bar{Y}_{i\bullet}) = \sigma^2 / n_i\):
\[\mathrm{Var}(\hat{\gamma}) = \sigma^2 \left(\frac{c_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{c_2^2}{n_2} + \cdots + \frac{c_I^2}{n_I}\right)\]
We estimate \(\sigma\) with the pooled standard deviation \(s_p\) from the full model, giving:
\[SE(\hat{\gamma}) = s_p \sqrt{\frac{c_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{c_2^2}{n_2} + \cdots + \frac{c_I^2}{n_I}}\]

title: Linear Combinations of Group Means
text:
Hypothesis Test
Under \(H_0\), the standardized estimate follows a \(t\) distribution:
\[t^* = \frac{\hat{\gamma}}{SE(\hat{\gamma})} \;\sim\; t_\nu, \quad \nu = n - I\]
The two-sided \(p\)-value is \(2 \cdot P(t_\nu \leq -|t^*|)\).

title: Linear Combinations of Group Means
text:
Confidence Interval
A 95% confidence interval for \(\gamma\):
\[\hat{\gamma} \;\pm\; t_\nu(0.975) \cdot SE(\hat{\gamma})\]

title: Linear Combinations of Group Means
text:
The Default Parameterization
Fitting a one-way ANOVA with aov() uses R’s default treatment-contrast parameterization: the model is \(Y_{ij} = \mu + \delta_i + \varepsilon_{ij}\) with \(\delta_1 = 0\) (the first factor level is the reference group). The coefficients returned are \(\hat{\mu}\) and \(\hat{\delta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\delta}_I\) — not the group means \(\mu_1, \ldots, \mu_I\).

aout_full <- aov(Percent ~ Judge, data = case0502)
coef(aout_full)

 (Intercept)       JudgeB       JudgeC       JudgeD       JudgeE       JudgeF 
  34.1200000   -0.5033333   -5.0200000   -7.1200000   -7.1533333   -7.3200000 
JudgeSpock's 
 -19.4977778 


The interpretation: (Intercept) = \(\hat{\mu}_A\) (judge A’s mean); JudgeB = \(\hat{\mu}_B - \hat{\mu}_A\); and so on. The group means are \(\hat{\mu}_i = \hat{\mu} + \hat{\delta}_i\), but this parameterization makes setting up coefficient vectors awkward.

title: Linear Combinations of Group Means
text:
Forcing the Group-Means Parameterization
Subtracting 1 from the formula removes the intercept and forces R to fit \(Y_{ij} = \mu_i + \varepsilon_{ij}\) directly, returning each group mean:

aout_lc <- aov(Percent ~ Judge - 1, data = case0502)
coef(aout_lc)

      JudgeA       JudgeB       JudgeC       JudgeD       JudgeE       JudgeF 
    34.12000     33.61667     29.10000     27.00000     26.96667     26.80000 
JudgeSpock's 
    14.62222 








WarningUse - 1 only for linearHypothesis()



The no-intercept fit gives correct group means, but it corrupts the ANOVA table: summary() and anova() will give wrong \(F\)-statistics and \(p\)-values. Only switch to ~ Judge - 1 immediately before calling linearHypothesis(). Use the ordinary ~ Judge fit for everything else.

title: Linear Combinations of Group Means
text:
Checking the Factor Level Order
The coefficient vector must be in the same order as the factor levels:

levels(case0502$Judge)

[1] "A"       "B"       "C"       "D"       "E"       "F"       "Spock's"


The order is A, B, C, D, E, F, Spock's — so position 7 is Spock’s judge.

title: Linear Combinations of Group Means
text:
Setting Up Coefficient Vectors
Test 1: Is Spock’s mean different from the average of the other six?
\[H_0: \mu_{\text{Spock's}} - \tfrac{1}{6}\mu_A - \tfrac{1}{6}\mu_B - \cdots - \tfrac{1}{6}\mu_F = 0\]

combo_vec1 <- c(-1/6, -1/6, -1/6, -1/6, -1/6, -1/6, 1)

Test 2: Is the average of judges A–C equal to the average of judges D–F?
\[H_0: \tfrac{1}{3}\mu_A + \tfrac{1}{3}\mu_B + \tfrac{1}{3}\mu_C - \tfrac{1}{3}\mu_D - \tfrac{1}{3}\mu_E - \tfrac{1}{3}\mu_F = 0\]

combo_vec2 <- c(1/3, 1/3, 1/3, -1/3, -1/3, -1/3, 0)

title: Linear Combinations of Group Means
text:
Running the Tests

lhout1 <- linearHypothesis(model = aout_lc, hypothesis.matrix = combo_vec1)
lhout1


Linear hypothesis test:
- 0.166666666666667 JudgeA - 0.166666666666667 JudgeB - 0.166666666666667 JudgeC - 0.166666666666667 JudgeD - 0.166666666666667 JudgeE - 0.166666666666667 JudgeF  + JudgeSpock's = 0

Model 1: restricted model
Model 2: Percent ~ Judge - 1

  Res.Df    RSS Df Sum of Sq      F    Pr(>F)    
1     40 3401.2                                  
2     39 1864.4  1    1536.8 32.146 1.489e-06 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1



lhout2 <- linearHypothesis(model = aout_lc, hypothesis.matrix = combo_vec2)
lhout2


Linear hypothesis test:
0.333333333333333 JudgeA  + 0.333333333333333 JudgeB  + 0.333333333333333 JudgeC - 0.333333333333333 JudgeD - 0.333333333333333 JudgeE - 0.333333333333333 JudgeF = 0

Model 1: restricted model
Model 2: Percent ~ Judge - 1

  Res.Df    RSS Df Sum of Sq      F  Pr(>F)  
1     40 2070.1                              
2     39 1864.5  1    205.68 4.3024 0.04471 *
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1


Strong evidence that Spock’s judge’s mean differs from the pooled mean of the other six (\(p < 0.001\)). No evidence that the first three non-Spock judges (A–C) differ from the last three (D–F) (\(p = 0.26\)).

title: Linear Combinations of Group Means
text:
Correspondence to the \(t\)-Test
linearHypothesis() returns an \(F\)-statistic. For a single linear combination, \(F = t^2\), so \(|t| = \sqrt{F}\):

sqrt(lhout1$F[2])

[1] 5.669737


We can verify this manually. First, compute \(\hat\gamma\) and \(SE(\hat\gamma)\):

sumdat <- case0502 |>
  group_by(Judge) |>
  summarise(ybar = mean(Percent), n = n())

gamma_hat <- sum(combo_vec1 * sumdat$ybar)

sp  <- sqrt(sum(aout_lc$residuals^2) / (nrow(case0502) - 7))
se_gamma <- sp * sqrt(sum(combo_vec1^2 / sumdat$n))

tstat <- gamma_hat / se_gamma

cat("gamma_hat =", round(gamma_hat, 3), "\n",
    "SE       =", round(se_gamma, 3), "\n",
    "t*       =", round(tstat, 3), "\n")

gamma_hat = -14.978 
 SE       = 2.642 
 t*       = -5.67 



2 * pt(-abs(tstat), df = nrow(case0502) - 7)

[1] 1.489078e-06

lhout1$`Pr(>F)`[2]

[1] 1.489078e-06


The \(p\)-values are identical, confirming the equivalence.

title: One-Way ANOVA
text: library(tidyverse)

title: One-Way ANOVA
section: Case Study 5.1.1: Diet Restriction and Longevity
text:
Case Study 5.1.1: Diet Restriction and Longevity
A series of studies found that restricting caloric intake can dramatically increase life expectancy in animals. In one randomized experiment, female mice were assigned at random to one of six diet groups:

title: One-Way ANOVA
section: Case Study 5.1.2: The Spock Conspiracy Trial
text:
Case Study 5.1.2: The Spock Conspiracy Trial
In 1968, Dr. Benjamin Spock was tried in Boston on charges related to encouraging draft resistance during the Vietnam War. The defense argued that the Spock trial judge (referred to here as “Spock’s” judge) systematically underrepresented women on jury venires. They compared this judge’s venires with those of six other Boston-area district judges (labeled A–F).
This is an observational study: judge assignment to cases was not randomized.
The response is Percent — the percentage of women on each venire. We are interested in:

Is Spock’s judge’s mean percent-women lower than the other judges?
Are the other judges’ means all the same?


ggplot(case0502, aes(x = Judge, y = Percent)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(y = "Percent women on venire")









ggplot(case0502, aes(x = Judge, y = Percent)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, height = 0) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "crossbar",
               color = "tomato", linewidth = 0.5, width = 0.55) +
  labs(y = "Percent women on venire",
       caption = "Red bars = group sample means")

title: One-Way ANOVA
text:
Pairwise Hypotheses
Test whether any specific pair of means differs: \[H_0: \mu_2 = \mu_5, \qquad H_A: \mu_2 \neq \mu_5\]
With \(I = 7\) groups there are \(\binom{7}{2} = 21\) possible pairwise tests.

title: One-Way ANOVA
text:
Omnibus Hypothesis (“Are any means different?”)
\[H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3 = \mu_4 = \mu_5 = \mu_6 = \mu_7\] \[H_A: \text{at least one } \mu_i \neq \mu_j\]

title: One-Way ANOVA
text:
One Group vs. the Rest
\[H_0: \mu_1 = \tfrac{1}{6}(\mu_2 + \mu_3 + \mu_4 + \mu_5 + \mu_6 + \mu_7)\] \[H_A: \mu_1 \neq \tfrac{1}{6}(\mu_2 + \mu_3 + \mu_4 + \mu_5 + \mu_6 + \mu_7)\]
This asks: Is Spock’s judge’s mean different from the average of the other judges?

title: One-Way ANOVA
text:
Group Sample Means
Estimate \(\mu_i\) with the group sample mean: \[\bar{Y}_{i\bullet} = \frac{1}{n_i}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i} Y_{ij}\]

title: One-Way ANOVA
text:
Pooled Variance
Each group has its own sample standard deviation \(s_i\), measuring the spread of observations within that group. Because we assume the same true variance \(\sigma^2\) for every group, we can combine these within-group estimates into one more accurate estimate. We weight each group by the amount of information it contributes — groups with more observations get more weight:
\[s_p^2 = \frac{(n_1-1)s_1^2 + (n_2-1)s_2^2 + \cdots + (n_I-1)s_I^2}
               {(n_1-1) + (n_2-1) + \cdots + (n_I-1)}\]
In words: each term \((n_i-1)s_i^2\) in the numerator is the sum of squared deviations from group \(i\)’s mean. Adding these across all groups gives the total within-group variation. Dividing by the total degrees of freedom gives the average within-group variance.
The denominator is \(\nu = n - I\) (the total degrees of freedom available for estimating variance within groups — we lose one degree of freedom per group because we estimated each group’s mean).
Two observations:

This is a weighted average of the within-group variance estimates, weighted by their degrees of freedom.
When \(I = 2\) this reduces exactly to the pooled variance from Chapter 2.

title: One-Way ANOVA
text:
Full and Reduced Models









Reduced model (\(H_0\))
Full model (\(H_A\))




Means
\(\mu_1 = \mu_2 = \cdots = \mu_I = \mu\) (one shared mean)
\(\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_I\) (separate means)


Estimates
\(\hat{\mu} = \bar{Y}_{\bullet\bullet}\)
\(\hat{\mu}_i = \bar{Y}_{i\bullet}\)


# parameters
1
\(I\)



Where \[
Y_{i\bullet} = \frac{1}{n_i}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}Y_{ij}
\] \[
\bar{Y}_{\bullet\bullet} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^I\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}Y_{ij},
\] \[
n = \sum_{i=1}^{I}n_i
\]
For the Spock data (\(I = 7\) judges), here is what each model predicts for each group:



Group
Judge
Full model prediction
Reduced model prediction




1
Spock’s
\(\bar{Y}_{1\bullet}\)
\(\bar{Y}_{\bullet\bullet}\)


2
Judge A
\(\bar{Y}_{2\bullet}\)
\(\bar{Y}_{\bullet\bullet}\)


3
Judge B
\(\bar{Y}_{3\bullet}\)
\(\bar{Y}_{\bullet\bullet}\)


4
Judge C
\(\bar{Y}_{4\bullet}\)
\(\bar{Y}_{\bullet\bullet}\)


5
Judge D
\(\bar{Y}_{5\bullet}\)
\(\bar{Y}_{\bullet\bullet}\)


6
Judge E
\(\bar{Y}_{6\bullet}\)
\(\bar{Y}_{\bullet\bullet}\)


7
Judge F
\(\bar{Y}_{7\bullet}\)
\(\bar{Y}_{\bullet\bullet}\)



In the full model, each group gets its own sample mean as a prediction. Spock’s judge’s venires are predicted by Spock’s judge’s average; Judge A’s venires are predicted by Judge A’s average; and so on. There are 7 free parameters — one per judge.
In the reduced model, every observation — regardless of judge — is predicted by the same grand average \(\bar{Y}_{\bullet\bullet}\) (the mean over all 46 venires). This is the model that says \(H_0\) is true: if all judges have the same expected percent-women, then the best single prediction for any venire is just the overall average.
What is a residual? A residual is simply the distance between an observed value and the model’s prediction for it:

Full model residual: \(Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i\bullet}\)

how far observation \((i,j)\) is from its group’s mean

Reduced model residual: \(Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{\bullet\bullet}\)

how far observation \((i,j)\) is from the grand mean


Intuitively, if the groups really have different means, the full model residuals will be small (each observation is close to its own group mean), while the reduced model residuals will be large (observations from Spock’s group, for example, are far from the overall grand mean).

title: One-Way ANOVA
text:
Sums of Squared Residuals
The residual sum of squares (RSS) for each model is:
\[RSS_{full} = \sum_i \sum_j (Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i\bullet})^2,
\quad df_{full} = n - I\]
\[RSS_{reduced} = \sum_i \sum_j (Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{\bullet\bullet})^2,
\quad df_{reduced} = n - 1\]

Each RSS adds up all the squared residuals across every observation.

a small RSS means predictions are close to the data
a large RSS means they are far away.
Both RSS values involve squaring (so positive and negative residuals don’t cancel) and summing (so we get a single overall measure).

The extra sum of squares (ESS) measures how much the fit improves when we go from the reduced model to the full model:
\[ESS = RSS_{reduced} - RSS_{full}, \quad df_{extra} = I - 1\]
NOTE: \(RSS_{reduced} \geq RSS_{full}\)

The full model has the reduced model as a special case: if all the \(\mu_i\) happen to be equal, the full model’s separate group means would all estimate the same value as the grand mean, and the two models would give identical fits. In general, the full model has more flexibility, so it can never fit worse than the reduced model. It can always do at least as well. Therefore \(ESS \geq 0\) always.

What does ESS tell us?

If \(H_0\) is true (all means really are equal), fitting separate group means should not improve the fit by very much — the data don’t contain strong evidence for distinct groups. In that case, ESS will be positive but small. If the means truly differ (the data come from groups with different means), the full model will fit much better, and ESS will be large. The \(F\)-test formalizes what “small” and “large” mean.


The plots below illustrate this for the Spock data. Red bars/lines show the model’s fitted means; larger gaps between points and those lines = larger residuals.









The squared residuals are much smaller under the full model, confirming that group means differ substantially.

title: One-Way ANOVA
text:
The \(F\)-Statistic
If \(H_0\) is true, both \(ESS/df_{extra}\) and \(RSS_{full}/df_{full} = s_p^2\) estimate the same \(\sigma^2\), so their ratio should be near 1. If \(H_A\) is true, the means differ and \(ESS\) will be inflated, pushing the ratio above 1.
\[F = \frac{ESS / df_{extra}}{s_p^2}
    = \frac{ESS / (I-1)}{RSS_{full} / (n-I)}\]
Under \(H_0\), \(F \sim F_{I-1,\; n-I}\). The \(p\)-value uses only the upper tail (large \(F\) is evidence against \(H_0\)).

title: One-Way ANOVA
text:
The \(F\)-Distribution
The \(F\) distribution is parameterized by two degrees of freedom: numerator (\(df_1\)) and denominator (\(df_2\)). Below are a few shapes:

title: One-Way ANOVA
text:
\(p\)-Value for the Spock Omnibus Test
The Spock data has \(I = 7\) judges and \(n = 46\) venires, so \(df_{extra} = 6\) and \(df_{full} = 39\).

n     <- nrow(case0502)
I     <- n_distinct(case0502$Judge)
df_extra <- I - 1          # 6
df_full  <- n - I          # 39
ess      <- rss_null - rss_full
sp2      <- rss_full / df_full
fstat    <- (ess / df_extra) / sp2
c(ESS = ess, RSS_full = rss_full, sp2 = sp2, F = fstat)

        ESS    RSS_full         sp2           F 
1927.080865 1864.445222   47.806288    6.718366 



pf(fstat, df1 = df_extra, df2 = df_full, lower.tail = FALSE)

[1] 6.095823e-05

title: One-Way ANOVA
text:
Checking the Grouping Variable
Before fitting, confirm the grouping variable is a factor or character:

class(case0502$Judge)

[1] "factor"


If it were numeric, aov() would treat it as a continuous predictor, not a grouping variable. Use as.factor() or mutate(Judge = factor(Judge)) to fix this.

title: One-Way ANOVA
text:
Omnibus \(F\)-Test with aov() and tidy()

aout_full <- aov(Percent ~ Judge, data = case0502)
tidy(aout_full)

text:

To test the intermediate hypothesis (Spock’s differs; others pooled):

aout_null         <- aov(Percent ~ 1,       data = case0502)
